title: Evacuation-chair readiness and operator boundary
summary: A pre-incident inspection and training card for evacuation chairs that protects the user, prevents untrained improvisation, and records storage, route, capacity, transfers, and maintenance.
content:
A pre-incident inspection and training card for evacuation chairs that protects the user, prevents untrained improvisation, and records storage, route, capacity, transfers, and maintenance.

Use the materials as a compact field kit. Work through the steps in order, pause whenever a warning or stop condition applies, and write down the outcome so another person can continue safely.
steps: Select equipment with the intended user and qualified accessibility or safety staff, confirming weight limits, body support, transfer method, staircase type, landings, doors, and final refuge.
Inspect seals, straps, tracks, wheels, handles, frame, storage mount, signage, and access at the manufacturer’s interval and after every use.
Train multiple operators and the user on communication, consent, transfer, posture, braking, turning, team commands, and what to do if the normal stair is blocked.
During an incident use only trained operators and approved routes, keep the user informed, stop for equipment damage or unsafe stairs, and reunite the person with their mobility device promptly.
warnings: Never use an evacuation chair for the first time during a real emergency.
Do not exceed manufacturer limits or carry the occupied chair on a route not approved in training.
common_mistakes: Starting Evacuation-chair readiness and operator boundary without first checking immediate danger and official instructions.
Failing to record time, location, responsible person, and unresolved risks for Evacuation-chair readiness and operator boundary.
alternatives: Use a paper-only version of Evacuation-chair readiness and operator boundary when power or connectivity is unavailable.
Assign one trained helper to simplify Evacuation-chair readiness and operator boundary when the group is stressed or short-handed.
failure_signs: Conditions become more dangerous while carrying out Evacuation-chair readiness and operator boundary.
Critical information cannot be confirmed or the assigned helper cannot continue Evacuation-chair readiness and operator boundary safely.
when_not_to_use: Do not continue Evacuation-chair readiness and operator boundary when it requires entering an unstable, contaminated, flooded, burning, electrically hazardous, or officially closed area.
Do not improvise medical, electrical, structural, or rescue actions beyond your training while using Evacuation-chair readiness and operator boundary.
short_term: ['Within the first operational period, Evacuation-chair readiness and operator boundary should produce a dated status, one responsible person, and a safe next action.', 'Repeat the essential check for Evacuation-chair readiness and operator boundary whenever conditions, official advice, or available resources change.']
long_term: ['Include lessons from Evacuation-chair readiness and operator boundary in the household or community emergency plan and resupply list.', 'Review the records from Evacuation-chair readiness and operator boundary after the event and correct gaps before the next emergency.']
if_method_fails: ['If Evacuation-chair readiness and operator boundary cannot be completed, move to the safest verified location, notify the responsible contact, and record what remains unresolved.', 'Use official emergency services or qualified specialists when the limits of Evacuation-chair readiness and operator boundary are reached.']
environment_notes: ['Adapt Evacuation-chair readiness and operator boundary to local terrain, weather, building type, transport access, language, disability needs, and official restrictions.', 'In Greece, use 112 for urgent emergencies and follow Civil Protection or local-authority instructions relevant to Evacuation-chair readiness and operator boundary.']
